subject: ap-chemistry
title: Thermodynamics & Gibbs Free Energy
stimulus:
A student wants to determine the concentration of $H_2O$
in a solution of $H_2O$(aq). The student can use one of
two titrants, either dichromate ion, Cr
$O_2$–(aq), or cobalt(II) ion, Co2+(aq). The balanced chemical equations
for the two titration reactions are shown below.
Dichromate as titrant: Cr $O_2$–(aq) + 3 $H_2O$(aq) + 8 H+(aq) → 2 Cr3+(aq) + 3 $O_2$(g) + 7 $H_2O$(l)
2 7 2 2 2 2
Cobalt(II) as titrant: 2 Co2+(aq) + $H_2O$(aq) + 2 H+(aq) → 2 Co3+(aq) + 2 $H_2O$(l)
2 2 2
The half-reactions and the E° values for the systems related to the titrations above are given in the following
table.
E° (V)
Half-Reaction
at 298 K
Co3+(aq) + e − → Co2+(aq) 1.84
$H_2O$(aq) + 2 H+(aq) + 2 e − → 2 $H_2O$(l) 1.77
2 2 2
Cr $O_2$− (aq) + 14 H+(aq) + 6 e − → 2 Cr3+(aq) + 7 $H_2O$(l) 1.33
2 7 2
$O_2$(g) + 2 H+(aq) + 2 e − → $H_2O$(aq) 0.70
2 2 2

parts.prompt:
Use the information in the table to calculate the following.
(i) E° for the reaction between Cr
$O_2$–(aq) and $H_2O$(aq) at 298 K
(ii) E° for the reaction between Co2+(aq) and $H_2O$(aq) at 298 K
parts.scoring_notes: Part (a)(i): Dichromate reaction: H$_2$O$_2$(aq) is oxidized (O is −1 → O$_2$ with O = 0), so H$_2$O$_2$ is oxidized (anode half-reaction reversed from the table). Cr$_2$O$_7^{2-}$ is reduced (cathode). $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}} = 1.33 - 0.70 = +0.63\text{ V}$. Part (a)(ii): Cobalt reaction: Co$^{2+}$ is oxidized to Co$^{3+}$ (anode reversed) and H$_2$O$_2$ is reduced. Wait — Co$^{3+}$/Co$^{2+}$ has the higher $E°$ (1.84 V), so Co$^{3+}$ is the oxidizing agent: Co$^{3+}$ reduced (cathode, E° = +1.84 V), H$_2$O$_2$ oxidized (anode, E° = +0.70 V reversed → cathode perspective = 1.77 V? Re-examine: H$_2$O$_2$ + 2H$^+$ + 2e$^-$ → 2H$_2$O, E° = 1.77 V as the reduction half-reaction; Co$^{3+}$ + e$^-$ → Co$^{2+}$, E° = 1.84 V as reduction. For the cobalt reaction: Co$^{2+}$ is OXIDIZED (anode), so we reverse the Co half-reaction: Co$^{2+}$ → Co$^{3+}$ + e$^-$ (−1.84 V); H$_2$O$_2$ is reduced (cathode, +1.77 V). $E°_{\text{cell}} = 1.77 - 1.84 = -0.07\text{ V}$. Negative E°: thermodynamically unfavorable. Part (a)(i): Cr$_2$O$_7^{2-}$ is reduced (cathode, E° = +1.33 V); H$_2$O$_2$ is oxidized to O$_2$ (E° for O$_2$/H$_2$O$_2$ = 0.70 V). $E°_{\text{cell}} = 1.33 - 0.70 = +0.63\text{ V}$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct E° for the dichromate titration reaction = +0.63 V: 1 point

required_elements: Dichromate is reduced (cathode, E° = +1.33 V); H$_2$O$_2$ is oxidized to O$_2$ (E° = +0.70 V for the O$_2$/H$_2$O$_2$ couple).
alternatives.correct_example:
In the dichromate titration: Cr$_2$O$_7^{2-}$ is reduced (gains electrons) and H$_2$O$_2$ is oxidized (loses electrons).
$E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}} = 1.33\text{ V} - 0.70\text{ V} = +0.63\text{ V}$

wrong_examples: Adding $E°$ values directly — $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}}$, not the sum.
Reversing the half-reactions — the substance with the higher reduction potential is reduced (cathode).
common_traps: $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}}$. The half-reaction with the higher reduction potential serves as the cathode (reduction).
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for correctly combining $E°$ values: $E°_{\text{cell}} = 1.33 - 0.70 = 0.63\text{ V}$.

sample_responses.response: Cr$_2$O$_7^{2-}$ is reduced (cathode, $E° = +1.33$ V); H$_2$O$_2$ is oxidized to O$_2$ (the O$_2$/H$_2$O$_2$ couple has $E° = +0.70$ V as a reduction, and is used as the anode here). $E°_{\text{cell}} = 1.33 - 0.70 = +0.63$ V.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies which species is oxidized and reduced, applies $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}}$ — the CB model answer.
Adds the two reduction potentials instead of subtracting; the convention is $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}}$ when both half-reactions are written as reductions.

scoring_points.description: For the correct E° for the cobalt titration reaction = −0.07 V: 1 point

required_elements: H$_2$O$_2$ is reduced (cathode, E° = +1.77 V); Co$^{2+}$ is oxidized to Co$^{3+}$ (anode, E° = +1.84 V for Co$^{3+}$/Co$^{2+}$ reduction).
alternatives.correct_example:
In the cobalt titration: Co$^{2+}$ is oxidized to Co$^{3+}$ and H$_2$O$_2$ is reduced to H$_2$O.
$E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}} = 1.77\text{ V} - 1.84\text{ V} = -0.07\text{ V}$

wrong_examples: Calculating 1.84 − 1.77 = +0.07 V — Co is oxidized (anode), not reduced. The oxidized species defines the anode.
Swapping cathode and anode assignments — H$_2$O$_2$ is reduced in this reaction; Co$^{2+}$ is oxidized.
common_traps: In the cobalt reaction, Co$^{2+}$ → Co$^{3+}$ means cobalt is OXIDIZED → anode. H$_2$O$_2$ → H$_2$O means H$_2$O$_2$ is REDUCED → cathode.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for correctly combining $E°$ values: $E°_{\text{cell}} = 1.77 - 1.84 = -0.07\text{ V}$.

sample_responses.response: For the cobalt titration, Co$^{2+}$ is oxidized to Co$^{3+}$ (anode, from reverse of Co$^{3+}$/Co$^{2+}$ reduction at +1.84 V) and H$_2$O$_2$ is reduced (cathode, +1.77 V). $E°_{\text{cell}} = 1.77 - 1.84 = -0.07$ V.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that Co$^{2+}$ is the reductant (oxidized) and H$_2$O$_2$ is the oxidant (reduced), then applies $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}}$ — the CB model answer.
Swaps cathode and anode — in the cobalt titration, Co$^{2+}$ is oxidized (anode) and H$_2$O$_2$ is reduced (cathode); the cathode potential (1.77 V) minus the anode potential (1.84 V) gives −0.07 V.

parts.prompt:
Based on the calculated values of E°, the student must choose the titrant for which the titration reaction is
thermodynamically favorable at 298 K.
(i) Which titrant should the student choose? Explain your reasoning.
(ii) Calculate the value of ΔG°, in kJ/mol
rxn
, for the reaction between the chosen titrant and $H_2O$(aq).
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): The dichromate titration has E° = +0.63 V > 0, meaning it is thermodynamically favorable (ΔG° < 0). The cobalt titration has E° = −0.07 V < 0, meaning it is thermodynamically unfavorable (ΔG° > 0). Choose dichromate. Part (b)(ii): For the dichromate reaction: Cr$_2$O$_7^{2-}$ + 3H$_2$O$_2$ + 8H$^+$ → 2Cr$^{3+}$ + 3O$_2$ + 7H$_2$O. Number of electrons transferred $n$ = 6. $\Delta G° = -nFE° = -(6)(96,485\text{ C/mol})(+0.63\text{ V}) = -364,900\text{ J/mol} \approx -365\text{ kJ/mol}$.

scoring_points.description: For choosing dichromate with E° > 0 thermodynamic reasoning: 1 point

required_elements: The dichromate reaction has E° = +0.63 V > 0, indicating ΔG° < 0 (thermodynamically favorable). The cobalt reaction has E° = −0.07 V < 0 (thermodynamically unfavorable).
alternatives.correct_example: Choose dichromate. From part (a), the dichromate reaction has $E° = +0.63\text{ V}$ and the cobalt reaction has $E° = -0.07\text{ V}$. A positive $E°$ means $\Delta G° < 0$ (spontaneous). A negative $E°$ means $\Delta G° > 0$ (nonspontaneous). Therefore, only the dichromate titration is thermodynamically favorable.

wrong_examples: Choosing cobalt — E° for the cobalt reaction is negative, indicating a nonspontaneous process.
Choosing cobalt because it has a larger E° for the Co$^{3+}$/Co$^{2+}$ half-reaction — the cell E° (not the half-reaction E°) determines spontaneity.
common_traps: Spontaneous reaction: $E°_{\text{cell}} > 0$ ↔ $\Delta G° < 0$. Use the cell E° (cathode − anode), not individual half-reaction E° values.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for choosing the correct titrant AND for understanding that a positive $E°$ or a negative $\Delta G°$ is required. The student should use the dichromate ion for the titration because $E°$ for that reaction is positive, meaning the reaction is thermodynamically favorable (or equivalently $\Delta G° = -nFE° < 0$).

sample_responses.response: Dichromate should be chosen. The dichromate titration has $E° = +0.63$ V > 0, so $\Delta G° = -nFE° < 0$ and the reaction is thermodynamically favorable. The cobalt titration has $E° = -0.07$ V, giving $\Delta G° > 0$ — not spontaneous.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the correct titrant and links the sign of $E°$ to the sign of $\Delta G°$ — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Cobalt should be chosen because Co$^{3+}$/Co$^{2+}$ has the highest half-reaction $E°$ (+1.84 V).
sample_responses.commentary: The spontaneity of the overall reaction is determined by $E°_{\text{cell}}$ (cathode − anode), not by any single half-reaction; the cobalt titration's $E°_{\text{cell}}$ is negative.

scoring_points.description: For the correct ΔG° calculation using $\Delta G° = -nFE°$ with n = 6: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
For the dichromate reaction: H$_2$O$_2$ → O$_2$ requires 2e$^-$ per H$_2$O$_2$; with 3 mol H$_2$O$_2$, $n = 6$ electrons total.
$\Delta G° = -nFE° = -(6)(96,485\text{ C/mol})(0.63\text{ V}) = -364,900\text{ J/mol} \approx -365\text{ kJ/mol}$

wrong_examples: Using n = 2 — the balanced dichromate equation shows 6 electrons transferred (Cr$_2$O$_7^{2-}$ gains 6, H$_2$O$_2$ loses 2 × 3).
Forgetting to convert J to kJ — divide by 1000 at the end.
common_traps: $n$ = total electrons transferred in the balanced overall equation. For the dichromate reaction, each Cr goes from +6 to +3 (3e$^-$ gain × 2 Cr = 6e$^-$), and each H$_2$O$_2$ loses 2e$^-$ (O: −1 → 0), so 3 × 2 = 6e$^-$ lost. Balanced: $n = 6$.
